Defendant Elon Boms' Demurrer To 3rd Amended Complaint
Off Calendar Nov-15-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Elon Boms' Demurrer To 3rd Amended Complaint.

Defendant Elon Boms' demurrer to the twenty-second cause of action in the third amended complaint for violation of the Unfair Competition Law is sustained with twenty days leave to amend to give plaintiff Thomas Sassani an opportunity, if he can do so in good faith, to allege in a UCL claim that Mr. Boms obtained money or property from Zozi as a result of an unlawful activity that Mr. Boms committed against Zozi. While Mr. Boms is technically correct that Mr. Sassani needed to obtain and did not have permission to add Mr. Boms to the UCL claim, there is no doubt that, had such permission been sought, it would have been granted, and thus Mr. Boms' argument about lack of permission is not a basis to sustain his demurrer. Neither Mr. Barth nor Mr. Boms has identified, nor is the court aware of, any published California authority requiring a UCL plaintiff to be a consumer or a competitor of the UCL defendant in a case based on the unlawful prong of the UCL. Imposing this requirement is inconsistent with the broad scope and language of the UCL and seems at odds with the case law permitting UCL claims based on a wide array of unlawful activity that can be committed against someone other than a consumer or a competitor. Liberally construed, the twenty-second cause of action adequately alleges that Zozi suffered economic injury as a result of Mr. Boms' unlawful activity, including Mr. Boms' causing Zozi to enter into economically unfavorable transactions. However, even liberally construed, the twenty-second cause of action fails to allege any facts supporting either an injunction or restitution against Mr. Boms in favor of Zozi and it appears that it is not reasonably possible that Mr. Sassani could allege any facts supporting injunctive relief against Mr. Boms.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/HK)

DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Nov-16-2017 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Attorney, Ravdeep S. Grewal and Nitoj P. Singh of the law offices of Dhillon Law Group, Inc. (415-433-1700) appear as counsela for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Alec H. Boyd of the law offices of Clyde & Co. US LLP (415-365-9800) appears as counsel for defendants/moving parties, Brian Barth and Ekoventure, Inc., dba ZOZI

Attorney, Stephanie Southwick of the law offices of Greenfield Draa & Harrington LLP (408-995-5600) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party, Elon Boms

No other appearance is made in this matter

The cause is argued and submitted by both sides as set forth in the transcript of the certified shorthand reporter.

The Court confirms the tentative ruling.

DEFENDANT ELON BOMS' DEMURRER TO FIRST AMENDED COMPLAINT, as to the first, fifth, ninth and thirteenth through sixteenth causes of action in the first amended complaint filed by plaintiff Thomas Sassani is overruled as to the first cause of action for declaratory relief, the fifth cause of action for tortious interference with contractual relations, and the ninth cause of action for workplace harassment and sustained without leave to amend as to the thirteenth through sixteenth causes of action for Labor Code violations. The declaratory relief claim is adequately alleged against Mr. Boms since Mr. Boms is alleged to be an agent of KLP and a person who worked in concert with and on behalf of KLP with regard to both the issuance of the promissory note and KLP's attempt to assert rights afforded to it in that note. The tortious interference claim is adequately alleged against Mr. Boms since, liberally construed, the FAC alleges that Mr. Boms interfered with Mr. Sassani's employment agreement for reasons other than for the benefit of Zozi. (Huynh v. Vu (2003) 111 Cal. App. 4th 1183). The harassment claim adequately alleges that Mr. Boms assertedly disability-based harassing conduct was both severe and pervasive. The Labor Code claims fail because Labor Code 558.1 does not create a private right of action against a director for an employer's violations of the Labor Code. Nothing in the language or the legislative history of section 558.1 states or suggests that it was intended to legislatively overrule the California Supreme Court case law that corporate agents are not liable to employees for a corporate employer's violations of the Labor Code. As made clear in the legislative history, section 558.1 was intended to provide an additional avenue of recourse to the Labor Commissioner to enforce judgments for Labor Code violations. Written order is signed and filed in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Laura Reding, CSR No. 9711, e-mail: lreding@sftc.org. (L&M302)

MINI MINUTES: No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT KLP ENTERPRISES, LLC'S Motion For Reconsideration is CONTINUED on the court's own motion to August 3, 2017 at 9:30 AM in Department 302. While it is questionable whether KLP acted with sufficient diligence in obtaining the information for which KLP seeks reconsideration to satisfy the diligence requirements of CCP 1008, the content of that information raises very serious questions whether the May 3 order denying KLP's motion to quash was correctly decided. The court exercises its discretion per Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal. 4th 1094 to reconsider on its own motion, without regard to the requirements of CCP 1008, whether the May 3 order was correctly decided. Now that the motion has been converted from a section 1008 motion to the court's own motion, due process suggests that the parties be afforded the opportunity to provide further briefing and the hearing is continued for that purpose. Plaintiff Thomas Sassani may file supplemental opposition papers no later than July 24, 2017 and KLP may file supplemental reply papers no later than July 28, 2017. For guidance of the parties, but not a limitation on their briefing, the court's views are: 1) the parties have sufficiently briefed whether KLP's contacts regarding the $5.7 million loan support specific personal jurisdiction over KLP, but 2) additional briefing might be warranted on whether KLP contacts other than in connection with the $5.7 million loan support specific personal jurisdiction over KLP as to any of Mr. Sassani's claims against KLP not based on the loan. Order is signed. Judge: Harold Kahn, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Carol Karen, CSR 711, ckaren@sftc.org =(302/HK)
